back_translation: Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, saying, "Jacob has taken away all that belonged to our father, and from what was our father's he has built all this wealth."
Jacob saw the face of Laban, and behold, it was not toward him as before.
The LORD said to Jacob, "Return to the land of your fathers and to your kindred, and I will be with you."
So Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field, to his flock,
and said to them, "I see your father's face is not toward me as before, but the God of my father has been with me.
You know that I have served your father with all my strength.
Your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times, but God did not allow him to harm me.
If he said, 'The speckled shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore speckled. If he said, 'The streaked shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore streaked.
God has taken away your father's livestock and given them to me.
When the flock was breeding, I saw in a dream that the male goats mating with the flock were streaked, speckled, and spotted.
The angel of God said to me in the dream, 'Jacob.' I said, 'Here I am.'
He said, 'Lift up your eyes and see — all the male goats mating with the flock are streaked, speckled, and spotted, for I have seen all that Laban has done to you.
I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and made a vow to me. Now arise, leave this land and return to the land of your birth.'"
Rachel and Leah answered him, "Do we still have any portion or inheritance in our father's house?
Are we not regarded by him as foreigners? For he has sold us and has consumed all our money.
All the wealth that God has taken from our father belongs to us and to our children. Now do whatever God has told you."
So Jacob arose and put his children and his wives on camels,
and he drove all his livestock and all his possessions that he had acquired, the livestock he had gained in Paddan-aram, to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
While Laban had gone to shear his sheep, Rachel stole the household idols belonging to her father.
Jacob deceived Laban the Aramean by not telling him that he was running away.
He fled with all that he had, arose and crossed the river, and headed toward the hill country of Gilead.
On the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
He took his relatives with him, pursued him a seven-day journey, and overtook him in the hill country of Gilead.
But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream of the night and said to him, "Be careful not to speak with Jacob from good to bad."
Laban overtook Jacob, and Jacob had pitched his tent in the mountain, and Laban with his relatives camped in the hill country of Gilead.
Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done? You have deceived me and carried off my daughters like captives of the sword!
Why did you flee secretly and deceive me, without telling me? I would have sent you away with joy and songs, with tambourine and harp.
You did not even let me kiss my sons and my daughters. Now you have done foolishly.
I have power in my hand to do you harm, but the God of your father said to me last night, 'Be careful not to speak with Jacob from good to bad.'
Now you have indeed gone because you longed for your father's house. But why have you stolen my gods?"
Jacob answered Laban, "I was afraid, for I thought you would seize your daughters from me by force.
With whomever you find your gods, that one shall not live. In the presence of our relatives, identify what is yours with me and take it." Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them.
Laban entered Jacob's tent, then Leah's tent, then the tent of the two maidservants, but did not find them. He went out from Leah's tent and entered Rachel's tent.
Rachel had taken the household idols, put them in the camel's saddlebag, and sat on them. Laban felt all about the tent but did not find them.
She said to her father, "Let it not anger my lord that I cannot rise before you, for the way of women is upon me." So he searched but did not find the household idols.
Then Jacob became angry and contended with Laban. Jacob answered and said to Laban, "What is my offense? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?
You have felt through all my goods. What have you found of all the goods of your house? Set it here before my brothers and your brothers, that they may judge between the two of us.
These twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and your goats have not miscarried, and the rams of your flock I have not eaten.
What was torn I did not bring to you. I bore the loss myself. From my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.
By day the heat consumed me, and by night the frost, and sleep fled from my eyes.
These twenty years I have been in your house. I served you fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your flock, and you changed my wages ten times.

Unless the God of my father — the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac — had been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. God has seen my affliction and the toil of my hands and judged last night."

Laban answered and said to Jacob, "The daughters are my daughters, the children are my children, the flock is my flock, and all that you see is mine. But what can I do today for these daughters of mine, or for their children whom they have borne?
Now then come, let us cut a covenant, you and I, and let it be a witness between you and me."
So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.
Jacob said to his relatives, "Gather stones." They took stones and made a heap, and they ate there by the heap.
Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob called it Galeed.
Laban said, "This heap is a witness between you and me today." Therefore its name was called Galeed,
and also Mizpah, because he said, "May the LORD watch between you and me when we are absent from one another.
If you mistreat my daughters or take wives besides my daughters, although no one is with us, see — God is witness between you and me."
Laban said to Jacob, "Behold this heap, and behold the pillar that I have set between you and me.
This heap is a witness, and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass over this heap to harm you, and you will not pass over this heap and pillar to harm me.
May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor — the God of their father — judge between us." So Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac.
Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and called his relatives to eat bread. They ate bread and spent the night on the mountain.
